findings.description: The NC Waste Management / Environmental Quality logo appears differently positioned in the HTML. In the PDF it is in the upper left corner with proper sizing; in the HTML it appears similarly placed but the overall header layout with the officials' names on the right is reformatted.
findings.evidence: PDF: Logo top-left with PAT MCCRORY, DONALD R. VAN DER VAART, MICHAEL SCOTT aligned right. HTML: Same elements present but layout is single-column stacked rather than side-by-side.

findings.description: The memo signature placement differs. In the PDF, the signature appears inline next to the From field. In the HTML, the signature appears as a separate block below the From field information.
findings.evidence: PDF: Signature is positioned to the right of 'Green Square Central Office / Division of Waste Management'. HTML: Signature image appears as a separate block below the From section.

findings.description: In the outline list, the PDF uses Roman numerals with a tab-indented format (I., II., III., IV.) while HTML renders them with periods and slightly different formatting, though the list structure is preserved.
findings.evidence: PDF: Indented Roman numeral list with clear tab stops. HTML: Roman numeral list with period-style formatting.

findings.description: The document title 'MEMORANDUM' is rendered as h1, which is acceptable. However, the main section headings (I, II, III, IV) are rendered as h2, which is correct. But the h1 uses both bold and underline inline styling rather than relying on the heading element alone — this is a stylistic choice, not a structural error. The more substantive issue is that the HTML uses multiple <main> elements, which is invalid HTML structure, but this is a structural/markup issue rather than a content fidelity issue.
findings.evidence: HTML has multiple <main> elements wrapping different page sections. This is an HTML validity issue but does not affect content fidelity.

findings.description: The footer text from page 1 appears both in the document header area and as a page-footer div. The PDF has this text once at the bottom of page 1, so displaying it once is sufficient. However, it also appears in the outer document wrapper at the very top of the HTML in the <head> area via the page-footer class, which is acceptable as the footer of page 1.
findings.evidence: Footer text 'State of North Carolina | Environmental Quality | Waste Management / 1646 Mail Service Center | 217 West Jones Street | Raleigh, 27699-1646 / 919 707 8200' appears in the header metadata AND as a page-footer div.
